title: Chapter 11: The New King's First Command
content: The heart-blood of a Viscount was nothing like the heart-blood of a Baron.

Kael had expected power. He got *transformation*. Dredd had been two hundred years old — two centuries of accumulated strength, stolen from the vampires he'd killed, the humans he'd drained, the territory he'd bled dry for tribute. All of it, compressed into a single dark jewel of blood that hit Kael's system like a meteor.

The first wave was pain. His bones didn't just shift — they *rebuilt*. His nerves lit up like they'd been doused in gasoline, every sensation amplified to the point of agony. He felt his fangs lengthen, his muscles thicken, his vision sharpen until he could see the individual dust motes suspended in the sunlight pouring through the shattered ceiling.

The second wave was expansion. His awareness exploded outward like a shockwave. He could feel every vampire in the Keep — every heartbeat, every drop of blood in their veins, every thread of loyalty or fear or calculation connecting them to their dead Viscount. He could feel the boundaries of Dredd's territory pressing against his mind, demanding recognition, and he *answered*. The territory was his now. Greyhaven was his. The blood markets, the barracks, the tunnels and chambers and ancient bone-cathedrals — all of it recognized him as its new master.

The third wave was memory. Not Dredd's memories — heart-blood didn't work that way. But *echoes*. Impressions. Fragments of the old vampire's will that clung to his power like oil to water. Dredd's paranoia. Dredd's obsession with tribute. Dredd's fear — sharp and bitter and buried deep — of a face Kael didn't recognize but somehow knew. A Prince's face. Ancient. Patient. Watching.
*Marius*, Kael thought. *Even the Rot King was afraid of Marius.*

When the transformation was complete, he stood in the ruined throne room with sunlight still pouring through the ceiling, ash still drifting through the air, and looked at the thirty-seven vampires who'd served Dredd for decades with new eyes.
Viscount's eyes.
"You have a choice," he said.

His voice had changed. Deeper. More resonant. It carried through the chamber with an authority that hadn't been there before — not just confidence, but *weight*. The weight of territory. The weight of rank. The weight of two hundred years of stolen power settling into its new vessel.

"Swear fealty to me, or leave Greyhaven by sunset. I won't kill you. The Blood Curse is real, and I'm not stupid enough to rack up marks on my first day as Viscount. But if you stay and betray me, I'll find a way around the Curse. I'm very good at finding ways around things."
The scarred Baron — Dredd's lieutenant, the one who'd protested when Kael had claimed the kill — was the first to kneel.
"Viscount Ashworth," he said. The words sounded like they hurt. "I, Baron Kestrel, pledge my service."

One by one, the others followed. Some knelt eagerly, their eyes gleaming with the opportunistic calculation of survivors who'd just found a stronger patron. Others hesitated, weighing decades of loyalty to a dead master against the very immediate reality of a Viscount who could walk in sunlight. In the end, they all knelt. Even the ones who hated him. Even the ones who were already planning to betray him.
When it was done, Kael had an army. A small one. A corrupt one. An army of vampires who'd spent their entire existence serving monsters and had no idea how to serve anything else.
But it was an army.
Marcus found him in Dredd's private quarters three hours later.

"I have to hand it to you," the blacksmith said, standing in the doorway. "When I drew those maps, I figured there was maybe a ten percent chance you'd actually go through with it. A one percent chance you'd survive. And a zero percent chance you'd walk out of that throne room as the new Viscount." He shook his head. "I've never been happier to be wrong."
"You helped make it happen."

"I drew lines on old invoices. You punched a ceiling until it broke." Marcus stepped into the room, his eyes moving over the obscene opulence of the Rot King's private space — silk sheets, blood fountains, walls lined with trophies from two hundred years of murder. "The city's already changing. Word spreads fast when a Viscount dies. The blood market's in chaos — half the merchants are packing to leave, the other half are trying to figure out how to ingratiate themselves with you. The humans are..." He paused. "The humans are scared. They've seen regime changes before. Usually, the new Viscount is worse than the old one."
"What do they need to hear?"

"That you're different. That the blood tithes are ending. That the Rot King's policies died with him." Marcus met his eyes. "But words are cheap. If you want the humans to trust you, you need to do something real. Something Dredd never would have done."

Kael thought for a moment. Then he walked to the blood fountain — a grotesque sculpture of intertwined bodies that pumped congealed blood in a continuous, slow-motion loop — and ripped it off the wall.
The fountain shattered against the floor. Decades-old blood splattered across silk sheets and bone trophies, a dark baptism that washed away two centuries of corruption.

"Tell the blood-bound they're free," Kael said. "Effective immediately. Any human who wants to leave Greyhaven will be escorted to the surface with supplies and protection. Any human who wants to stay will be paid for their labor. Actual wages. Not blood credits."
"And the vampires who resist?"

"Tell them the new Viscount has a very simple policy on resistance." Kael's smile was cold. "I can walk in sunlight. They can't. If they want to test which of us is more dangerous during the day, they're welcome to try."
Marcus stared at him for a long moment. Then he laughed — the same rough, surprised laugh Kael had heard in his shop.
"Twelve years," the blacksmith said. "Twelve years I've been waiting for someone like you."
He turned to leave. At the door, he paused.

"There's something else. Your paladin — Sera. She's in the old library, three levels down. She said to tell you... she found something. Something important." Marcus's expression darkened. "She looked scared, Viscount. And I've never seen that woman scared before."
Kael was already moving.
